It makes you feel quite low, like one of those grey days when the rain just won't stop. For someone like me, who prefers the solid, honest feeling of a well-loved novel or the rhythm of a good game of calcio, this manufactured drama is exhausting. I think the main thing is to treat these screens like a window, not like the whole world. You look out, you see what's there, but you don't feel compelled to live inside the noise. That is where the calm is. It is about building a small, protected garden of truth around yourself, filtering out the weeds of falsehood, even if it means missing out on some of the fleeting, frantic chatter. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: It is such a tiresome business, this constant barrage of nonsense on these digital platforms. When I sit down to look at the news, or even just to see what my friends are up to, it feels like wading through thick, muddy water. All this fake content, it just weighs on the spirit, doesn't it? It makes you feel quite low, like one of those grey days when the rain just won't stop. For someone like me, who prefers the solid, honest feeling of a well-loved novel or the rhythm of a good game of calcio, this manufactured drama is exhausting. I think the main thing is to treat these screens like a window, not like the whole world. You look out, you see what's there, but you don't feel compelled to live inside the noise. Since my education is not as advanced as some of the youngsters, I find that I have to rely on a slower, more careful way of looking at things. If something seems too dramatic, or if it makes you feel that immediate, sharp pang of anger or fear, it is a big warning sign. It is rarely true. I try to remember that the world I know, the one in Bologna with its real cobblestones and real people, is far more solid than anything flashing on a screen. I limit my time, of course, because dwelling on these things only makes the melancholy settle deeper. Instead, I try to seek out the genuine threads—a picture of a beautiful Tuscan landscape, a thoughtful comment from someone I trust, or perhaps a well-written piece about a painting. That is where the calm is. It is about building a small, protected garden of truth around yourself, filtering out the weeds of falsehood, even if it means missing out on some of the fleeting, frantic chatter. It is a slow process, but it is the only way to keep one's peace of mind when the digital world is such a chaotic mess.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 81-year-old Italian woman with limited formal education, prone to depression, and moderately extraverted. Her low conscientiousness suggests a less proactive, more reactive approach to problems. The tone should be weary, reflective, and slightly melancholic, aligning with her depressive feature and age. The advice must be practical for someone with limited digital literacy but strong personal intuition (derived from her life experience and moderate extraversion). The response focuses on emotional filtering (the 'feeling' test) rather than technical fact-checking, which suits her background.
